messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"avl-gate-forecloses-large-operator-channel","title":"The Year-1 primary channel sits behind an approved-vendor-list gate that pre-launch entrants historically cannot open in 12 months","narrative":"The venture's stated go-to-market is 'large operators first', and the funnel gives that buyer 40% of PDU spend. But large CE operators procure rack power through approved-vendor lists whose onboarding — factory audit, firmware security review, reference installs, dual-region logistics — is a qualification cycle, not a sales cycle. The incumbent lock is visible in Vertiv's named major-customer roster (Equinix, Telefonica, Vodafone). For a pre-launch entrant, the 0.40 slice is not 'ours to win slowly'; it is procedurally unreachable inside the model's Year-1 clock, and the YAM is keyed on exactly this buyer. The reachable Year-1 mix is the distributor/OEM tail the plan hasn't built.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large","obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Large-operator procurement requires AVL qualification (security audit, factory audit, references) that a pre-launch vendor cannot present on day 1; incumbents already hold these slots per Vertiv's named large-account roster","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c9","profile:Stage","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The 0.40 buyer-mix figure rests on a misread company-level order stat, not a channel study — nothing in it encodes time-to-qualify; meanwhile the indirect channels that ARE open to a new entrant (master distributor, OEM white-label) carry only 0.13 and 0.22 in the mix","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c0","digest:cust-mix#c8","ledger:cust.distributor","ledger:cust.oem"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The large-operator cell is foreclosed for the Year-1 window: excluding cust.operator-large removes the largest buyer slice from reachable scope and the YAM built on it, rather than merely shrinking it","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"'Large operators buy direct and are the biggest slice' reads as a strength, and the buyer-mix node looks like a market fact. The ledger has no node for time-to-qualify, so the risk is invisible in every sensitivity band — the mix is right about the market and wrong about the venture's first 12 months.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"A signed vendor-qualification or framework agreement with at least one CE large operator (colo or hyperscale) within the first two quarters, or documented evidence that a comparable new PDU vendor cleared a large-operator AVL in under 6 months.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"Multi-quarter vendor qualification is the documented norm for critical-power hardware at large accounts, and the venture starts with zero references; but some colos run fast-track pilot tracks that could open one account inside the year.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"Large-operator direct channel foreclosed for the Year-1 window — unreachable, not merely slow"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Stated supplier-onboarding lead time and prerequisites (audits, references, insurance) for new hardware vendors","where":"Equinix, Digital Realty and Vantage EMEA supplier-registration portals; procurement annexes in TED-listed DC fit-out tenders","threshold":">6 months from registration to RFQ eligibility, or mandatory reference installs","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Preferred-vendor or sole-source language for rack power at large CE accounts","where":"Vertiv/Schneider EMEA partner-program documents and quarterly earnings commentary on framework agreements","threshold":"Multi-year rack-power frameworks announced at ≥2 flagship CE operators","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run 3 structured CE operator procurement interviews focused specifically on AVL cycle length and entry prerequisites for a new PDU vendor","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"Directly replaces the internally-estimated 0.40 with an evidence-based reachable share and a qualification clock"},{"action":"Re-sequence Year-1 GTM through master-distributor and OEM white-label channels while running large-operator qualification in parallel","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts the foreclosed slice into a Year-2 target instead of a Year-1 dependency"},{"action":"Negotiate a paid pilot MOU with one mid-size CE colo that contractually feeds its AVL process","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Buys a reference install — the scarcest qualification asset"}]},{"id":"som-window-semantics-mismatch","title":"The 3% Year-1 obtainable is a 3–5-year benchmark wearing a 12-month clock","narrative":"The obtainableFactor was 'triangulated' from generic SOM heuristics — but the benchmark sources themselves disagree on what period the percentage describes. Wise defines SOM as a 3–5-year capture window; Prospeo and IdeaPlan frame 1–3 years. Applied to a single pre-launch year, the same literature implies numbers an order of magnitude lower: TryBuildCo's own worked example lands at ~0.1% of SAM, and the one real industrial hardware entrant found (Tractian) started at ~$200K against a $14.9B market. The model's 0.01–0.05 band brackets the wrong quantity — it brackets a multi-year share, not a first-12-months share for a company that starts the year without certs, channel, or references.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The benchmarks behind the 3% mid-band describe 1–3 or 3–5 year capture windows, not the first 12 months the profile specifies","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c1","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c4","profile:Stage"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"When the same sources are read at the Year-1 grain, observed and worked-example first-period shares fall to 0.1% of SAM and below — orders of magnitude under the curated band's 1% floor","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c5","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c6","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c7"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"obtainableFactor 0.03 overstates Year-1 capture by roughly 5x; YAM scales linearly with it while TAM/SAM stay untouched","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The node was already revised DOWN (from 6% to 3%) and is labelled 'triangulated' with two agreeing sources — it looks like the conservative, well-handled assumption. The flaw is not the number's level but the time-unit mismatch between the benchmark's window and the model's, which no sensitivity band expresses.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Any named DC-hardware or comparable industrial B2B entrant with a documented first-12-months revenue at or above 1% of its stated SAM in a market with incumbent CR3 near 0.55.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"Every source that quantifies a real first period (TryBuildCo example, Tractian) sits far below 1%, and the digests found zero sector-specific Year-1 benchmarks supporting 3%; countervailing: AI-driven demand growth may lift new-entrant win rates above historical analogues.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.006,"note":"Year-1 obtainable re-anchored on first-period analogues rather than multi-year SOM heuristics"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Months from launch to first paid PO for new rack-power/DC-hardware entrants in EMEA","where":"Press releases and case studies of recent PDU/rack-power market entrants; Data Centre Dynamics vendor-launch coverage","threshold":">9 months median to first PO","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Time for a new vendor to appear on CE electrical-distributor line cards after launch","where":"Rexel, Sonepar and regional CE distributor published line cards / new-vendor announcements","threshold":">2 quarters to first listing","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Replace the percentage heuristic with a capacity-based bottom-up model (named accounts × win rate × sales-cycle length × deployment capacity) before committing the Year-1 plan","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Prospeo itself recommends this over the percentage heuristic; the digests confirm no one has instantiated it for DC hardware"},{"action":"Structure Year-1 targets and cash plan around a 0.5–1% obtainable case, treating 3% as upside rather than base","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Prevents the ramp-dependent hiring and inventory commitments that make an overstated YAM fatal rather than embarrassing"}]},{"id":"compliance-serialization-zeroes-h1","title":"A serialized cert-and-listing critical path zeroes H1 revenue — 'conservative ramp' is not linear in months","narrative":"The YAM implicitly assumes the selling machine exists on day 1 of the 12-month window. But an intelligent PDU sold into CE must first clear a compliance stack the model only gestures at: CE (LVD/EMC), RoHS, WEEE producer registration (Germany's stiftung EAR is a named, dated gate), EORI customs registration, and the buyer-side pull-through of EU 2024/1364 (DC rating scheme, in force May 2024) and EED 2024/1791 KPI disclosures that operators now push down into vendor questionnaires. These gates are serialized — test slots before declaration, registration before listing, listing before quote. A two-quarter slip does not scale YAM by 50% linearly; it deletes the ramp's compounding front end, because the conservative ramp back-loads revenue into quarters that no longer exist.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The real EU entry stack is materially larger than the three regimes the ledger cites: WEEE, ROHS, FCC, EORI customs, plus the 2024 DC rating scheme and EED disclosure duties landing on operators and flowing into vendor requirements","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c10","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c8","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c9"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Each gate precedes quoting eligibility; for a pre-launch venture the qualification clock and the harvest clock are the same 12 months, so a 2-quarter serialization consumes the highest-leverage half of the obtainable window","refs":["profile:Stage","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c6","ledger:serviceableFactor"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Effective Year-1 obtainable share halves or worse relative to the curated 0.03, independent of demand — the market is there, the vendor is not yet legally/commercially present in it","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"Compliance appears in the model as a static haircut inside serviceableFactor — a level, not a schedule. Because the funnel has no time axis, a delay risk masquerades as an already-priced reach discount, and 'conservative ramp' in the profile reads as if it already absorbs slippage.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A complete, dated compliance dossier (CE declaration, RoHS/WEEE registrations incl. stiftung EAR number, EORI) plus at least one distributor or operator listing in hand before the Year-1 clock starts.","likelihood":{"value":0.55,"rationale":"First-time EU market entry for networked electrical hardware routinely takes 2–4 quarters across test-lab lead times and national registrations; the venture is pre-launch with no evidence in the ledger that any of these artifacts exist yet.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.5,"note":"Two quarters of the Year-1 window consumed by serialized cert/registration/listing before first PO"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"WEEE producer-registration processing time for new foreign entrants in Germany","where":"stiftung EAR (stiftung-ear.de) published processing notes and applicant guidance","threshold":">10 weeks from application to registration number","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Test-slot lead times for LVD/EMC certification of power distribution hardware","where":"TÜV Rheinland / TÜV Nord lab booking lead-time notices to vendors","threshold":">8 weeks to first available slot","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a compliance-gap audit mapping every required artifact (per target country incl. non-EU Switzerland) to a dated critical path, before finalizing the Year-1 revenue plan","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Converts an unmodelled schedule risk into a plannable critical path; directly de-risks the Year-1 capture assumption"},{"action":"Start certification and national registrations in parallel now, pre-launch, treating the cert dossier as a launch gate rather than a post-launch task","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Moves the serialization out of the harvest window entirely"},{"action":"Contract a fulfilled-by-distributor arrangement where an established CE distributor's registrations (WEEE/EORI) cover initial shipments","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Rents another firm's compliance stack for the first two quarters"}]},{"id":"serviceable-reach-is-flow-not-stock","title":"serviceableFactor 0.55 is a stock; a pre-launch entrant only owns a flow — Year-1 reach is realistically two countries, one channel","narrative":"The 0.55 serviceable share treats channel and regulatory reach as an endowment. But reach is BUILT during the same 12 months it is supposed to be harvested: distributor contracts signed, country registrations obtained, support commitments staffed, five languages covered. The only quantified external analogues the research found for a realistically serviceable slice are 12% (Scalarly's regional-narrowing example) and single-digit SOM ranges — nothing supports 45–65% for a pre-launch vendor. Concretely: Switzerland (8% of TAM) is non-EU and needs its own conformity path; Poland and 'Other CE' need local-language channel that doesn't exist yet; even in Germany, coverage means signed distributors, not the country's existence. A pre-launch Year-1 footprint of DE plus NL through one distributor is closer to 0.30–0.35 of TAM than 0.55.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor","geo.CH"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The 0.55 is an unanchored assumption leaf: every external quantified analogue found sits far lower (12% regional-narrowing example; investor preference for bottom-up over top-down reach claims), and no bottom-up channel-coverage model exists in the ledger","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c3","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13","ledger:serviceableFactor"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Reach must be constructed country-by-country inside the Year-1 window: SAM is a capability statement (compliance, integrations, support model, channel) and each element is unbuilt at launch; non-EU Switzerland (8% of the geo split) and the CEE tail require separate conformity and channel work","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c12","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c10","ledger:geo.CH","ledger:geo.other"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Year-1 serviceable share falls from 0.55 toward a two-country, one-channel footprint; SAM contracts proportionally and YAM with it, even if the obtainable percentage of that SAM holds","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:geo.DE"]}],"whyMissable":"The 0.55 carries a wide, symmetric band (0.45–0.65) that looks like honest uncertainty about a level. What's hidden is that for a pre-launch venture the true Year-1 value starts near zero and ramps — the band brackets the steady state, not the first year, and the funnel's lack of a time axis makes the two indistinguishable.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Signed distribution or direct-coverage agreements plus completed country registrations covering ≥55% of the CE-7 TAM base (by geo weight) within the first quarter after launch.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"Judgment anchored on the digests' finding that no external evidence supports 45–65% reach for any comparable vendor and that reach components (certs, channel, support) verifiably do not exist pre-launch; offset by the possibility of a single pan-CE master distributor deal covering multiple countries at once.","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"set","value":0.35,"note":"Year-1 realistic footprint: DE+NL via one distributor plus partial direct coverage; CH and CEE tail unbuilt"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Count and geographic coverage of signed CE distribution agreements at launch","where":"Vendor's own channel announcements cross-checked against Rexel/Sonepar and regional CE distributor line cards","threshold":"<2 countries covered by signed agreements at launch","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Swiss conformity path status for the product (non-EU, post-MRA friction for networked electrical equipment)","where":"Swiss authorized-representative and conformity guidance (SECO / Swissmem publications) plus the vendor's own compliance roadmap","threshold":"No Swiss authorized rep appointed by launch","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Build the bottom-up channel-coverage model (named accounts × reachable share per country) that the node's own promotion path calls for, and re-derive serviceableFactor from it","type":"information","voiNodeId":"serviceableFactor","note":"The single highest-value replacement of an unanchored assumption in the SAM step"},{"action":"Prioritize one pan-CE master distributor with existing DE/NL/PL coverage over country-by-country channel building","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Buys multi-country reach as one contract instead of five"},{"action":"Defer Switzerland and the CEE tail out of the Year-1 plan explicitly, and rebase the YAM on the DE/NL footprint","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Aligns the model's reach with the venture's actual Year-1 build plan"}]},{"id":"firmware-audit-and-poc-attrition","title":"An intelligent PDU is a networked device: the security-audit gate precedes every listing, and a third of deployed pilots never convert to accepted revenue","narrative":"'Intelligent' is the product's differentiator and its hidden tax. A networked PDU with remote management enters the buyer's attack surface, so operators audit the vendor's firmware-signing, vulnerability-disclosure and update infrastructure BEFORE listing — a gate that precedes the first PO and appears nowhere in the funnel. The venture must stand up this infrastructure (secure OTA, SBOM, disclosure policy) during the same year it is supposed to harvest. And clearing the gate isn't the end: POC-stage industrial hardware shows ~30% of deployed systems never reaching acceptance — customers sign, vendors deploy, and the hardware comes back months later. Both effects hit realized Year-1 revenue after the demand, the reach, and even the PO exist, which is why no market-sizing band captures them.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Buyer security review of networked power hardware (firmware update path, SBOM, disclosure process) is a pre-listing gate for the intelligent-PDU product class; the pre-launch venture has no audited update infrastructure on day 1","refs":["profile:Product","profile:Stage","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c12"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Deals that clear the gate still attrit: ~30% of deployed POC-stage industrial systems are never fully accepted, so booked pilots overstate realized revenue by a large margin in the first year","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c8","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c9"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Realized Year-1 obtainable share is materially below the pipeline-implied share even at constant win rates — obtainableFactor overstates what converts to accepted, invoiced revenue inside the window","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"Security review and pilot acceptance live downstream of everything a market model measures — demand, reach, even signed POs. The founder's risk list covers 'can we sell it'; this risk is 'does what we sold count', and the surface signal (a signed pilot) actively reassures. The intelligence features driving the value proposition are the same ones creating the gate.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Two CE operator or colo security reviews passed pre-launch (documented sign-off on the firmware/update stack), plus first pilot cohort showing >90% acceptance-to-invoice conversion within two quarters of deployment.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"The 30% non-acceptance figure is a documented POC-stage base rate for industrial hardware, and networked-device security gating at DC operators is standard practice; uncertainty is whether a rack PDU's blast radius triggers full-depth audits at mid-size buyers or only at large operators.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.7,"note":"Pilot-to-accepted-revenue attrition plus security-gate delays erode realized Year-1 capture by ~30%"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Cybersecurity requirements (SBOM, signed firmware, vulnerability-disclosure policy) appearing as mandatory line items in DC fit-out and rack-power RFQs","where":"TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) DC procurement notices; large-operator supplier security questionnaires","threshold":"Security annex mandatory in ≥half of sampled rack-power RFQs","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Regulatory hardening of connected-device requirements applicable to networked PDUs (RED cybersecurity delegated act enforcement; Cyber Resilience Act transition dates)","where":"Official Journal of the EU / Commission standards dockets on RED 2022/30 and CRA implementation","threshold":"CRA conformity dates confirmed inside the venture's Year-1 window","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Survey the actual security-review requirements of 5 target CE buyers (questionnaires, audit depth, pass criteria) before finalizing the launch feature set","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Establishes whether the gate is questionnaire-deep or audit-deep per buyer tier — the difference is a quarter of runway"},{"action":"Build and third-party-attest the firmware-signing/OTA/SBOM stack pre-launch, and package the attestation as a sales asset","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Turns the gate into a differentiator against slower-moving incumbents' legacy firmware practices"},{"action":"Write acceptance criteria and conversion milestones into every pilot contract (defined test period, auto-conversion clause, partial payment on deployment)","type":"contractual","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Directly attacks the 30% never-accepted attrition mode"}]}]}
